title: Recap: Final Presentation
text:
Extracting Years from Descriptions

netflix_titles <- netflix_titles |>
  mutate(year_mentions = str_extract_all(description, "\\b(19|20)\\d{2}\\b"))

# - \\b: Matches a word boundary to ensure we're matching whole words.
# - (19|20): Matches either "19" or "20", representing the start of a year in the 1900s or 2000s.
# - \\d{2}: Matches exactly two digits, completing the year (e.g., 1999, 2020).
# - \\b: Ensures the match is a whole word, avoiding partial matches within longer strings.

title: Recap: Final Presentation
Recap: Final Presentation
text:
Identifying Titles with Repeated Words

netflix_titles <- netflix_titles |>
  mutate(repeated_words = str_detect(title, "\\b(\\w+)\\b.*\\b\\1\\b"))
# - \\b: Matches a word boundary to ensure we're matching whole words.
# - (\\w+): Captures a sequence of word characters (letters, digits, or underscores) as a group.
# - \\b: Ensures the captured group is a whole word.
# - .*: Matches any number of characters (except newlines) between the first and second occurrence of the word.
# - \\b\\1\\b: Matches the same word captured in the first group, ensuring it's a whole word.


# Count titles with repeated words
repeated_word_counts <- netflix_titles |>
  count(repeated_words)

# Display the counts
repeated_word_counts

title: Fast Food
text:
fastfood <- read_csv("data/fastfood_calories.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

avg_cal <- fastfood %>%
  group_by(restaurant) %>%
  summarise(mean_calories = mean(calories, na.rm = TRUE),
            n_items = n()) %>%
  arrange(desc(mean_calories))

avg_cal %>%
  slice_head(n = 10) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(restaurant, mean_calories),
             y = mean_calories)) +
  geom_col(fill = "tomato") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Top 10 Fast Food Chains by Average Calories per Menu Item",
    x = "Restaurant",
    y = "Average Calories"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

title: Fast Food
text:
Analysis:
This chart shows the fast-food chains with the highest average calories per menu item, highlighting meaningful differences across major brands. McDonald’s, Sonic, and Burger King top the list, each averaging over 600 calories per item—indicating that their menus tend to feature larger portions or more calorie-dense foods.
Restaurants like Arby’s, Dairy Queen, and Subway fall in the mid-range (roughly 520–560 calories), suggesting a mix of lighter and heavier options. At the lower end of the top ten, Taco Bell and Chick-fil-A average under 450 calories, indicating comparatively lighter items on average.
Overall, the data shows substantial variation across chains, with some offering consistently higher-calorie options. This may reflect differences in menu composition (e.g., fried foods, desserts, portion sizes), target customers, or brand positioning within the fast-food market.

title: Fifa Data
text:
Analysis:
The scatterplot reveals a strong positive relationship between a country’s share of the world population and its share of the FIFA World Cup TV audience. Most points lie above the red dashed 45-degree line, indicating that many countries have a disproportionately high TV audience relative to their population share. This suggests that smaller countries, in terms of population, often punch above their weight in terms of World Cup viewership. Confederations also show distinct patterns, with some regions consistently outperforming others in audience share relative to population.

title: Project 2: Netflix Text Analysis
text:
This report analyzes Netflix movie and TV show descriptions using text mining techniques. The dataset, accessed from TidyTuesday (2021-04-20), includes thousands of titles from a variety of genres and regions, from the start of Netflix to 2019. My goal is to uncover patterns in naming and description practices that reveal thematic or marketing trends.

netflix_titles <- readr::read_csv(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2021/2021-04-20/netflix_titles.csv"
)



We begin by checking how common it is for Netflix titles to contain numbers (e.g., 13 Reasons Why, 3%). This could reflect stylistic choices aimed at emphasizing uniqueness or mystery.

netflix_titles <- netflix_titles |>
  mutate(has_number = str_detect(title, "\\d+"))

table(netflix_titles$has_number)


FALSE  TRUE 
 7361   426 

prop.table(table(netflix_titles$has_number))


     FALSE       TRUE 
0.94529344 0.05470656 

#Here, we use the `str_detect()` function with the regular expression `\\d+` to check whether each title contains a **number** (like *13 Reasons Why* or *3%*).  
#This tells us how common numeric titles are — a pattern that can reflect marketing or thematic choices.

#The table above shows how many Netflix titles contain numbers compared to those that do not.')

The results show that only 426 out of 7,787 Netflix titles (about 5.5%) contain a number, while the remaining 94.5% do not. This tells us that numeric titles are relatively rare in Netflix’s catalog. When they do appear, they tend to be used deliberately—often to signal a countdown (3%), a sequence (Part 2), an age or milestone (13 Reasons Why), or some other device meant to catch attention or provide context.




netflix_titles <- netflix_titles |>
  mutate(
    love_mentions    = str_count(description, "\\b(?i)love\\b"),
    death_mentions   = str_count(description, "\\b(?i)death\\b"),
    mystery_mentions = str_count(description, "\\b(?i)mystery\\b")
  )

theme_counts <- netflix_titles |>
  summarise(
    love = sum(love_mentions, na.rm = TRUE),
    death = sum(death_mentions, na.rm = TRUE),
    mystery = sum(mystery_mentions, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(), names_to = "theme", values_to = "count") 

ggplot(theme_counts, aes(x = theme, y = count, fill = theme)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(
    title = "Common Themes in Netflix Descriptions",
    x = "Theme",
    y = "Count of Mentions",
    fill = "Theme"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()








Interpretation:
The first table shows the count of titles that do or do not contain a number. TRUE indicates the title contains at least one digit.
The plot compares the frequency of the words “love,” “death,” and “mystery” across all Netflix descriptions.
We can see that “love” dominates, revealing that romantic themes are heavily used.
“Death” and “mystery” occur less often, but still signal important narrative categories.
This visualization illustrates how simple word counting can reveal broad narrative trends.
Finding Adjectives That Appear Before “Story” (Lookaround Example)

story_words <- netflix_titles |>
  mutate(adj_before_story = str_extract(description, "\\b\\w+(?= story)")) |>
  filter(!is.na(adj_before_story)) |>
  mutate(adj_before_story = tolower(adj_before_story)) |>
  anti_join(stop_words, by = c("adj_before_story" = "word")) |>
  count(adj_before_story, sort = TRUE)

head(story_words, 10)

# A tibble: 10 × 2
   adj_before_story     n
   <chr>            <int>
 1 true                48
 2 love                 8
 3 life                 5
 4 origin               3
 5 biblical             2
 6 crime                2
 7 dark                 2
 8 fictional            2
 9 age                  1
10 american             1


Here we use a regular expression with a lookahead — (?= story) — to extract the word immediately before “story” in each description.
For example, in a phrase like “an incredible story”, the pattern will capture “incredible.”
Interpretation: Looking at the top 10 words preceding story, it is clear that “true” is a clear winner. After a sharp fall off, love, life and origin take places 2-4. This trend reveals that almost all of the words before ‘story’ are adjectives describing the story.
Most Frequent Words in Descriptions (Excluding filler)

data("stop_words")

top_words <- netflix_titles |>
  unnest_tokens(word, description) |>
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_head(n = 15)

ggplot(top_words, aes(x = reorder(word, -n), y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Most Frequent Words in Netflix Descriptions",
    x = "Word",
    y = "Frequency"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))











This bar chart shows the 15 most common non-stop words found in Netflix descriptions.
Words like “life,” “family,” “world,” and “love” appear prominently — reinforcing the emotional and human-centered tone of Netflix’s catalog.
This kind of frequency analysis helps identify how streaming services position their content around universal, relatable ideas.






netflix_titles <- netflix_titles |>
  mutate(year_mentions = str_extract_all(description, "\\b(19|20)\\d{2}\\b"))

# Count the frequency of years
year_counts <- netflix_titles |>
  unnest(year_mentions) |>
  count(year_mentions, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) # Select top 10 years

# Plot the most frequently mentioned years
ggplot(year_counts, aes(x = reorder(year_mentions, -n), y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "darkgreen") +
  labs(
    title = "Top 10 Most Frequently Mentioned Years in Descriptions",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Frequency"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))












netflix_titles <- netflix_titles |>
  mutate(repeated_words = str_detect(title, "\\b(\\w+)\\b.*\\b\\1\\b"))

# Count titles with repeated words
repeated_word_counts <- netflix_titles |>
  count(repeated_words)

# Display the counts
repeated_word_counts

# A tibble: 2 × 2
  repeated_words     n
  <lgl>          <int>
1 FALSE           7560
2 TRUE             227

title: Project 2: Netflix Text Analysis
text:
Summary of Insights
From these visual and textual analyses, we can draw several conclusions:

Titles with numbers are relatively common.
The word “love” overwhelmingly dominates thematic keywords, suggesting that romantic and emotional themes are heavily emphasized.

Descriptions frequently include adjectives like “true” or “untold” before “story,” reflecting Netflix’s use of authenticity and intrigue in marketing language.

The most common descriptive words — life, family, world — reveal that Netflix’s storytelling appeals to broad, shared human experiences.

title: Josh Goldhaber
text: I am Josh Goldhaber, a sophomore at Pitzer College majoring in Data Science at Claremont McKenna College

title: Traffic Stops with SQL
text:
This project analyzes traffic-stop data from three agencies in the Stanford Open Policing Project (Pierson et al. 2020): New Orleans Police Department (LA), Madison Police Department (WI), and Louisville Metro Police Department (KY). All data wrangling is done in SQL. R is used only to summarize the SQL output and produce figures.
I focus on two questions:

How do citation and warning rates vary across race within each city?
How do these patterns differ across jurisdictions?

Because the three agencies store outcomes differently, the key step is to harmonize the outcome definitions (especially for New Orleans) and then stack the agencies into one consistent dataset that can be grouped and compared.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(DBI)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

con_traffic <- DBI::dbConnect(
  RMariaDB::MariaDB(),
  dbname = "traffic",
  host = Sys.getenv("TRAFFIC_HOST"),
  user = Sys.getenv("TRAFFIC_USER"),
  password = Sys.getenv("TRAFFIC_PWD")
)




Before building the combined dataset, I confirm table names available in the database. I also extract the state prefix from each table name using SUBSTRING_INDEX, which makes it easier to locate relevant datasets quickly.


Code
SELECT 
  table_name,
  SUBSTRING_INDEX(table_name, '_', 1) AS state
FROM information_schema.tables
WHERE table_schema = DATABASE()
ORDER BY state, table_name;



Displaying records 1 - 10


table_name
state




ar_little_rock_2020_04_01
ar


az_gilbert_2020_04_01
az


az_mesa_2023_01_26

az_statewide_2020_04_01

ca_anaheim_2020_04_01

ca_bakersfield_2020_04_01

ca_long_beach_2020_04_01

ca_los_angeles_2020_04_01

ca_oakland_2020_04_01

ca_santa_ana_2020_04_01
ca





From this list, I focus on three city-level datasets: la_new_orleans_2020_04_01, wi_madison_2023_01_26, and ky_louisville_2023_01_26.



The block below creates one unified dataset by stacking the three city tables using UNION ALL and adding a city label. New Orleans stores outcome as text, so I use CASE WHEN to create comparable binary indicators for citation_issued and warning_issued. Madison and Louisville already contain numeric flags with those names, so I select them directly. I filter to rows with non-missing subject_race because race is essential for the comparisons that follow.


Code
SELECT
  city,
  subject_race,
  COUNT(*) AS stops,
  AVG(citation_issued) AS citation_rate,
  AVG(warning_issued) AS warning_rate
FROM (
    -- New Orleans: recode text outcome into harmonized binary indicators
    SELECT
        'New Orleans' AS city,
        subject_race,
        subject_sex,
        date,
        time,
        CASE WHEN outcome = 'citation' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS citation_issued,
        CASE WHEN outcome = 'warning' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS warning_issued
    FROM la_new_orleans_2020_04_01
    WHERE subject_race IS NOT NULL

    UNION ALL

    -- Madison: indicators already provided as numeric flags
    SELECT
        'Madison' AS city,
        subject_race,
        subject_sex,
        date,
        time,
        citation_issued,
        warning_issued
    FROM wi_madison_2023_01_26
    WHERE subject_race IS NOT NULL

    UNION ALL

    -- Louisville: indicators already provided as numeric flags
    SELECT
        'Louisville' AS city,
        subject_race,
        subject_sex,
        date,
        time,
        citation_issued,
        warning_issued
    FROM ky_louisville_2023_01_26
    WHERE subject_race IS NOT NULL
) AS three_city
GROUP BY city, subject_race
ORDER BY city, subject_race;

From this list, I focus on three city-level datasets: la_new_orleans_2020_04_01, wi_madison_2023_01_26, and ky_louisville_2023_01_26.

title: Traffic Stops with SQL
text:
The block below creates one unified dataset by stacking the three city tables using UNION ALL and adding a city label. New Orleans stores outcome as text, so I use CASE WHEN to create comparable binary indicators for citation_issued and warning_issued. Madison and Louisville already contain numeric flags with those names, so I select them directly. I filter to rows with non-missing subject_race because race is essential for the comparisons that follow.


Code
SELECT
  city,
  subject_race,
  COUNT(*) AS stops,
  AVG(citation_issued) AS citation_rate,
  AVG(warning_issued) AS warning_rate
FROM (
    -- New Orleans: recode text outcome into harmonized binary indicators
    SELECT
        'New Orleans' AS city,
        subject_race,
        subject_sex,
        date,
        time,
        CASE WHEN outcome = 'citation' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS citation_issued,
        CASE WHEN outcome = 'warning' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS warning_issued
    FROM la_new_orleans_2020_04_01
    WHERE subject_race IS NOT NULL

    UNION ALL

    -- Madison: indicators already provided as numeric flags
    SELECT
        'Madison' AS city,
        subject_race,
        subject_sex,
        date,
        time,
        citation_issued,
        warning_issued
    FROM wi_madison_2023_01_26
    WHERE subject_race IS NOT NULL

    UNION ALL

    -- Louisville: indicators already provided as numeric flags
    SELECT
        'Louisville' AS city,
        subject_race,
        subject_sex,
        date,
        time,
        citation_issued,
        warning_issued
    FROM ky_louisville_2023_01_26
    WHERE subject_race IS NOT NULL
) AS three_city
GROUP BY city, subject_race
ORDER BY city, subject_race;

title: Traffic Stops with SQL
section: Figure 1: Citation rates by race and city
text:
Figure 1: Citation rates by race and city


Code
ggplot(race_city_summary, aes(x = city, y = citation_rate, fill = subject_race)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  labs(
    title = "Citation Rates by Race Across Three Cities",
    x = "Race",
    y = "Citation Rate (Proportion of Stops)",
    fill = "City"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))









Figure 1 shows substantial cross-city differences in how often traffic stops result in citations. Overall,New Orleans has consistently lower citation rates across race categories (roughly in the 20%–40% range), while Madison and Louisville are much higher for most groups (often around 70%–85%), suggesting different enforcement practices or recording conventions across jurisdictions.
Within-city racial patterns also vary. In Madison, citation rates are relatively similar across race categories (a fairly “flat” profile), whereas Louisville shows more dispersion, including notably low citation rates for the “other” category and extremely high rates for “unknown.” The “unknown” race category should be interpreted cautiously, since it can reflect missing/uncoded records and may involve smaller sample sizes; a useful follow-up is to compare the stop counts (stops) for each race–city group to see whether these extremes are driven by low-N categories.

title: Traffic Stops with SQL
section: Figure 2: Warning rates by race and city
text:
Figure 2: Warning rates by race and city


Code
ggplot(race_city_summary, aes(x = city, y = warning_rate, fill = subject_race)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  labs(
    title = "Warning Rates by Race Across Three Cities",
    x = "Race",
    y = "Warning Rate (Proportion of Stops)",
    fill = "City"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))









Figure 2 shows that warning rates vary more across cities than across racial groups. Madison has the highest warning rates across most races, generally around the high-20% to low-30% range. New Orleans is typically slightly lower but still comparable, while Louisville tends to have the lowest warning rates for most categories. Within each city, differences by race are generally modest. The main exception is Louisville’s “other” category, which appears extremely high (around 80%). This kind of spike is often driven by small sample sizes or differences in how race categories are coded, so it should be interpreted cautiously without checking the number of stops in that group.
Finally, the “unknown” category appears only for New Orleans in this figure, suggesting that missing or unknown race values are coded differently across agencies (or are not present after filtering) and therefore may not be directly comparable across cities.

title: Project 4: Ethics
text:
Northpointe’s COMPAS algorithm, implemented across courts of the United States with the intent of more accurately predicting recidivism rates, scores defendants with a “risk rating”. These scores range from one to ten, with one being unlikely to reoffend, and ten being highly likely to reoffend. The scores are based on 137 questions either answered by defendants or pulled from records. Notably, race is not one of those questions. However, there are concerns about the algorithm’s fairness, particularly surrounding race. ProPublica, a nonprofit investigative journalism organization, conducted a study, finding that the algorithm incorrectly flagged Black defendants as future criminals at nearly twice the rate of white defendants, while white defendants were more frequently mislabeled as low risk compared to Black defendants. Its fairness among women and men is also questioned: women at a risk rating of six are just as likely to reoffend as a man at a risk rating of 4.
This scenario illustrates a major ethical dilemma at the intersection of data science and the criminal justice system. COMPAS is intended to bring statistical rigor and reduce human bias in judicial decisions, but the ProPublica findings suggest that the model may unintentionally reproduce inequalities that are present in the data used to train it. Since the algorithm is proprietary and not fully transparent, neither defendants nor judges can clearly understand how risk scores are produced or whether they treat different groups equitably. The central ethical issue is whether it is appropriate or fair to use a predictive model with limited transparency to influence high stakes decisions such as bail, sentencing, and parole, especially when errors appear to fall disproportionately on certain racial and demographic groups.

title: Project 4: Ethics
text:
Northpointe’s COMPAS algorithm, implemented across courts of the United States with the intent of more accurately predicting recidivism rates, scores defendants with a “risk rating”. These scores range from one to ten, with one being unlikely to reoffend, and ten being highly likely to reoffend. The scores are based on 137 questions either answered by defendants or pulled from records. Notably, race is not one of those questions. However, there are concerns about the algorithm’s fairness, particularly surrounding race. ProPublica, a nonprofit investigative journalism organization, conducted a study, finding that the algorithm incorrectly flagged Black defendants as future criminals at nearly twice the rate of white defendants, while white defendants were more frequently mislabeled as low risk compared to Black defendants. Its fairness among women and men is also questioned: women at a risk rating of six are just as likely to reoffend as a man at a risk rating of 4.
This scenario illustrates a major ethical dilemma at the intersection of data science and the criminal justice system. COMPAS is intended to bring statistical rigor and reduce human bias in judicial decisions, but the ProPublica findings suggest that the model may unintentionally reproduce inequalities that are present in the data used to train it. title: Project 4: Ethics
text:
Question Response

What is the permission structure for using the data? Was it followed?
In the COMPAS example, there are two layers of permission and consent to consider. Courts in places like Broward County adopted COMPAS as part of their routine decision making for bail and sentencing, so defendants did not choose to have a proprietary risk score used to influence their punishment (Angwin et al., 2016). The risk score simply became part of the standard judicial process. Defendants also did not meaningfully consent to having their data used in later fairness audits. ProPublica obtained records through public-records requests, which is legally permitted but does not reflect informed consent from the people whose information was analyzed (Angwin et al., 2016). There is also a broader question raised by scholars: can people meaningfully consent to predictive uses of their data that do not even exist yet? As Andrew Lee Park notes, predictive systems in sentencing often rely on information that defendants never agreed to provide for this purpose, creating “a fundamental mismatch between the collection of data and its downstream uses” (Park, 2019).
Defendants also did not give specific consent for their data to be used in subsequent research audits of the algorithm. ProPublica obtained the data via public records, which is legally permissible, but nonetheless different from participants being clearly informed in advance that their information would be used to probe racial bias in a risk tool and then shared publicly as a dataset. In that sense, informed consent, especially for unforeseen downstream uses of the data, was very limited.


How were the variables collected? Were they accurately recorded? Is there any missing data?
The variables used in the Northpointe COMPAS algorithm dataset were collected through a combination of defendant questionnaires and information pulled from criminal justice records. The COMPAS tool uses 137 items that include factors such as prior arrests, family history with incarceration, school disciplinary issues, employment status, and peer associations, and these responses are combined with demographic information like age and gender (Angwin et al., 2016). Because defendants provide some of this information during the stressful and coercive environment of pretrial processing, there is a possibility that certain responses may be incomplete or inaccurate. ProPublica’s reporting also notes that Northpointe would not disclose exactly how all variables were weighted or whether the company performed internal validation of the completeness of the data it collected (Angwin et al., 2016). Scholars have raised concerns that variables taken from justice system records can embed existing racial and socioeconomic inequalities, and that many questionnaires rely on self-reported information that may be inconsistently recorded (Park, 2019). While ProPublica used two years of follow-up data to identify whether individuals were charged with new crimes, any cases with unclear or unavailable outcomes would be treated as missing, which introduces further uncertainty regarding data accuracy and completeness.


Who was measured? Are those individuals representative of the people to whom we’d like to generalize / apply the algorithm? Should we analyze data if we do not know how the data were collected?
The population examined in the ProPublica analysis of COMPAS consisted of more than 7,000 individuals who were arrested in Broward County, Florida, in 2013 and 2014 and whose cases moved through that county’s criminal justice system (Angwin et al., 2016). Importantly, this dataset reflects only the people included in ProPublica’s study—not the full national population of defendants evaluated by the COMPAS algorithm. Because the ProPublica dataset is drawn from a single county, it is a narrow and highly specific sample shaped by the policing patterns, demographics, and local judicial practices of Broward County, rather than a representative sample of everyone who receives COMPAS scores across the United States.
While COMPAS is used in many jurisdictions with different crime patterns, populations, and institutional conditions, the ProPublica dataset is local and historically contingent, making it an imperfect proxy for evaluating the tool’s broader performance (Park, 2019). Park notes that algorithms or assessments evaluated using such constrained datasets may behave unpredictably when generalized beyond the context in which the data were collected.
This raises a methodological concern: to what extent can fairness claims be inferred from a dataset when the underlying data-generation process is unclear or context-dependent? If we do not fully understand the policing behaviors, arrest practices, or conditions under which defendants completed survey items in Broward County, then any conclusions drawn from the ProPublica dataset—including claims about COMPAS fairness—become more limited and less reliable (Angwin et al., 2016).


Should race be used as a variable? Is it a proxy for something else (e.g., amount of melanin in the skin, stress of navigating microaggressions, zip-code, etc.)? What about gender?
Race is not explicitly used as a variable in the COMPAS questionnaire, but ProPublica’s investigation shows that the algorithm still produces outcomes that differ sharply by race, which strongly suggests that other inputs act as proxies (Angwin et al., 2016). Variables such as prior arrests, neighborhood stability, family incarceration, and school discipline history are all deeply shaped by structural racism and patterns of policing, so including them can indirectly encode racial disparities even without asking about race directly. Scholars at UCLA Law argue that many of the inputs used in predictive risk tools function as “functional proxies for race” because they reflect unequal social conditions rather than individual behavior (Park, 2019). This means that excluding race does not prevent racial bias, and including race would raise its own ethical problems because it would allow courts to make decisions partly on the basis of a protected characteristic. Gender also plays an important role as touched on in my introduction to the algorithm. ProPublica found that women and men with the same risk score do not have the same likelihood of reoffending, which suggests inconsistent calibration across gender groups (Angwin et al., 2016). The core concern is that both race and gender can become encoded in the model through the correlations present in the data, so even when they are not included explicitly, the algorithm may still learn patterns that reflect social inequities rather than genuine risk.
In my opinion, I believe that first of all, there should not be an algorithm that determines your chance of recidivism at all, and most definitely not an algorithm that has any say in your sentencing.
The following is an excerpt from my paper: “Examining the Permissibility of Statistical Discrimination in COMPAS-Based Sentencing”, which I wrote for my Data Science Ethics course taught by Bradley McHose, expanding on this viewpoint.
The use of an algorithm in general, as a replacement of a judge is impermissible because each defendant should be tried based on the entirety of the facts and information relevant to their case instead of just statistical correlation. To exemplify this argument, I present a case in which the use of COMPAS removed the influence of the court. Paul Zilly, sentenced on February 15, 2023, was convicted of stealing tools and a lawn mower. During the sentencing hearing, the prosecution advised the judge to sentence Zilly to one year in county jail and post-incarceration supervision. The judge however, citing a high risk rating produced by Northpointe’s algorithm, refuted the advised punishment and imposed two years in state prison with three years of supervision. The judge’s choice to rule almost exclusively based on the algorithm removed the effect of qualitative elements about Zilly that would have been considered if the algorithm had not been used. For example, the algorithm recognized that Zilly had struggled with drug usage in the past, correlating that with high likelihood to reoffend, however neglected all of the changes that Zilly has made to stop using drugs and better his life. Its usage of statistical discrimination in this case and others might cause many to see this as grounds for its impermissibility, as it removes the humanitarian aspect of the legal process and devalues the qualitative analysis conducted by lawyers at trial. 
Not only does this render the judge a ‘rubber stamp’, it undermines the legal system in its essence: redirecting its function from a system that aims to rehabilitate defendants into a machine that determines sentencing based on their likelihood of committing a future crime. 
// END EXCERPT
Aside from the ethics of the use of an algorithm in the first place, the incorporation of race or gender into the algorithm should not be allowed, for many reasons. The most prominent reason of them all is the confounding effect. Because the results of the algorithm are determined by questions related to ancestral or familial jail time and previous sentences, the algorithm can have a snowball effect, where historical patterns of over-policing and structural discrimination become encoded as indicators of individual risk. This means that people from communities that have faced disproportionate surveillance or harsher charging practices may receive higher risk scores simply because the model treats the consequences of past injustice as predictive signals. As scholars note, many variables in risk assessments function as indirect stand-ins for race, which allows the model to reproduce racial disparities even when race is not explicitly included (Park, 2019). ProPublica shows that this dynamic leads to Black defendants being falsely labeled as high risk at nearly twice the rate of white defendants, which demonstrates how quickly seemingly neutral inputs can magnify unequal outcomes (Angwin et al., 2016). By allowing attributes tied to race or gender to influence predictions, the algorithm reinforces existing inequalities and turns social disadvantage into justification for harsher legal decisions.


Were the data made publicly available? Why? How? On what platform?
A central issue highlighted in the COMPAS investigation is that Northpointe never disclosed how the algorithm actually generates its risk scores. ProPublica found that the company would not reveal the formula or the weight placed on any of the 137 variables, calling the model proprietary and declining to share the scoring method with the public or with the courts that rely on it (Angwin et al., 2016). Because of this secrecy, neither defendants nor judges can evaluate how the algorithm reaches its conclusions or whether the inputs are combined in a fair or statistically sound way. UCLA Law notes that this lack of transparency turns COMPAS into a “Black box” that shapes sentencing and bail decisions while hiding its internal logic from the very people it affects (Park, 2019). With no access to the method, it becomes impossible to determine whether the racial disparities observed in the outcomes stem from biased data, flawed modeling choices, or assumptions built into the algorithm. This closed design limits accountability and makes it difficult to challenge or correct errors in a system that directly influences a person’s freedom.
In terms of ProPublica’s research, they made the COMPAS dataset publicly available so that outside researchers, journalists, and policymakers could independently examine whether the algorithm was producing racially unequal outcomes. ProPublica obtained the original records through public records requests to Broward County and then released a cleaned and documented version of the dataset on its website, along with a detailed methodology explaining how the analysis was conducted. The choice to share the data publicly reflects a belief that systems influencing sentencing and bail decisions should be open to public scrutiny, especially when they are known to affect vulnerable groups.

title: Project 4: Ethics
text:
Conclusion (Question 4)
The issues described above matter because they shape who benefits from the use of COMPAS and who is placed at greater risk of harm. When an algorithm is built on data that may be inaccurately collected, drawn from a narrow and unrepresentative group, and influenced by variables that operate as proxies for race or gender, the resulting predictions do not distribute error evenly. ProPublica’s findings show that the burden of these errors falls most heavily on Black defendants, who were far more likely to be incorrectly labeled as high risk despite not reoffending (Angwin et al., 2016). Those who benefit are typically the institutions that gain efficiency or maintain authority by relying on a proprietary system that appears objective on the surface. UCLA Law argues that this reliance reinforces existing power structures because the algorithm’s design remains hidden, which prevents meaningful public oversight and allows court systems to treat its outputs as neutral science even when they reflect structural inequalities (Park, 2019). The neglect is experienced by defendants who are scored by a tool they did not meaningfully consent to, trained on data they did not control, and applied using a method they are not permitted to examine. The ethical violations involved are not simply accidental but serve institutional interests in efficiency, control, and risk management. The secrecy surrounding the model protects the company’s proprietary claims, and the opaque nature of the system strengthens the power of courts and corrections agencies by allowing them to justify decisions using an unchallengeable algorithm. The result is a system in which fairness is secondary to administrative convenience, and the people most affected by the tool have the least ability to contest it.

text:
Citations
Angwin, J., Larson, J., Mattu, S., & Kirchner, L. (2016, May 23). Machine bias: There’s software used across the country to predict future criminals. And it’s biased against Blacks. ProPublica. https://www.propublica.org/article/machine-bias-risk-assessments-in-criminal-sentencing

title: About This Website
text:
Welcome to my data science portfolio website! This site serves as a comprehensive showcase of my work, projects, and insights in the field of data science. Whether you are a fellow data enthusiast, a potential collaborator, or simply curious about the fascinating world of data, I hope you find the content here engaging and informative.


The primary goal of this website is to document and share my journey in data science. It includes a variety of projects that demonstrate my skills in data analysis, visualization, statistical modeling, and machine learning. Each project is accompanied by detailed explanations, code snippets, and visualizations to provide a clear understanding of the methodologies and insights derived.






Permutation Testing: An in-depth analysis of gold and silver price volatility using permutation tests to uncover differences in underlying market dynamics.
Netflix String Data Analysis: A text analysis project exploring patterns in Netflix titles using regular expressions and thematic visualizations.
FIFA Audience Insights: A statistical exploration of FIFA World Cup TV audience data, highlighting trends and relationships.
Fast Food Calories: A comparative analysis of calorie content across popular fast-food chains, visualized for easy interpretation.
SQL Data Analysis: An exploration of traffic-stops data with SQL.
Ethics Analysis: A comprehensive overview of the ethicality of the Northpointe COMPAS algorithm, including statistical evidence and even a personal paper on the subject.





This website is built using Quarto, a powerful tool for creating dynamic and interactive documents. The projects and analyses leverage a variety of R packages, including: - tidyverse: For data manipulation and visualization. - ggplot2: For creating elegant and informative plots. - infer: For statistical inference and hypothesis testing. - quantmod: For financial data analysis.

title: Permutation Testing: Are Silver’s Factors the same as Gold (August–October)?
text:
In this project, I investigate whether there is a fundamental difference in the underlying factors driving the prices of gold and silver.
Gold and silver are both precious metals often used as investment hedges against inflation and market risk, but their price behaviors differ: gold functions more as a monetary store of value, while silver has heavy industrial demand. Silver has historically traded with higher volatility. Although, in recent months, there has been momentum in both metals due to macroeconomic uncertainty.
My analysis aims to determine if there remains a fundamental difference in the factors influencing their price volatility. By examining differences in volatility, measured by standard deviation, my permutation test seeks to reveal whether these differences provide evidence of distinct underlying market dynamics for gold and silver.
Using daily price data from Yahoo Finance (tickers GC=F for gold and SI=F for silver), I compute daily log returns and perform a one-tailed permutation test under the null hypothesis that the two metals have equal volatility.
Because financial returns are often non-normal, a permutation test is a robust, simulation-based way to test this difference without assuming normality.



We compare the standard deviations of daily log returns for gold and silver.

Null Hypothesis (H₀): Gold and Silver are driven by the same market factors and their difference in volatility can be explained by chance and randomness of markets.
Alternative Hypothesis (H₁): The underlying market dynamics for Silver lead to greater daily price variability than Gold that can’t be explained by randomness or chance.

Instead of relying on a theoretical distribution (like a t-test or F-test), we use a permutation test, which simulates what differences we’d expect purely due to chance under the null hypothesis.
We look at market volatility and price to conclude wether or not these two metals are influenced by the same factors.




We use quantmod to pull daily futures data from Yahoo Finance and tidyverse for data wrangling. Log returns are computed using dailyReturn(..., type = "log") for stability and symmetry.


Code
library(quantmod)
library(tidyverse)
library(infer)
library(purrr)

# Download daily futures prices (August 2025 – Present)
GC_F <- getSymbols("GC=F", src = "yahoo", from = "2025-08-01", to = Sys.Date(), auto.assign = FALSE)
SI_F <- getSymbols("SI=F", src = "yahoo", from = "2025-08-01", to = Sys.Date(), auto.assign = FALSE)

# Compute log returns
gold_ret <- dailyReturn(Cl(GC_F), type = "log")
silver_ret <- dailyReturn(Cl(SI_F), type = "log")

# Combine and reshape to long format
returns_long <- tibble(
  date = index(gold_ret),
  Gold = as.numeric(gold_ret),
  Silver = as.numeric(silver_ret)
) |>
  drop_na() |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(Gold, Silver), names_to = "Metal", values_to = "Return")






This plot shows the raw time series of daily log returns for gold and silver.
It helps the reader visually compare volatility over time — wider fluctuations suggest higher variance.


Code
ggplot(returns_long, aes(x = date, y = Return, color = Metal)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(
    title = "Daily Log Returns for Gold and Silver (August–Present)",
    x = "Date", y = "Daily Log Return",
    color = "Metal"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









Analysis:
We observe that silver (in blue) exhibits more frequent and larger daily swings compared to gold.
This visual pattern suggests that silver may indeed have higher volatility — which we now test more formally.




This function performs one simulation of the null hypothesis by randomly shuffling the Metal labels.
This breaks the link between returns and metal type, simulating a world where there is no real difference in volatility.


Code
# This function simulates one permutation of the null hypothesis
simulate_diff_sd <- function(df) {
  df |>
    mutate(Metal = sample(Metal)) |>
    group_by(Metal) |>
    summarise(sd = sd(Return), .groups = "drop") |>
    summarise(diff_sd = diff(sd)) |>
    pull(diff_sd)
}






We compute the actual observed difference in standard deviations — this is our test statistic.
A positive value means silver is more volatile than gold.


Code
obs_diff <- returns_long |>
  group_by(Metal) |>
  summarise(sd = sd(Return), .groups = "drop") |>
  summarise(diff_sd = diff(sd)) |>
  pull(diff_sd)

obs_diff


[1] 0.01050667


Interpretation:
This number captures how much larger silver’s return variability is, compared to gold’s.
We’ll compare this observed difference to the null distribution next.




We repeat the permutation process 1000 times using map_dbl() to create a distribution of SD differences expected under the null hypothesis (i.e., if metals were equally volatile). The map_() function allows us to repeat this process smoothly and efficiently.


Code
set.seed(47)

# Perform 1000 permutations under the null hypothesis
sim_diffs <- map_dbl(1:1000, ~simulate_diff_sd(returns_long))

# Store in tibble for plotting
sim_df <- tibble(diff_sd = sim_diffs)






This histogram shows the null distribution of SD differences.
The red dashed line represents the actual observed difference.
If it’s far to the right, that provides evidence that silver is truly more volatile.


Code
ggplot(sim_df, aes(x = diff_sd)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "skyblue", color = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = obs_diff, color = "red", linetype = "dashed", size = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Permutation Distribution of Difference in Volatility (Silver − Gold)",
    subtitle = "Red dashed line shows the observed difference",
    x = "Difference in SD under Null (permuted)",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









Interpretation:
The observed value lies far in the upper tail of the distribution — suggesting that such a large difference would be very unlikely by chance if the metals were equally volatile.




We calculate the proportion of permuted SD differences that are greater than or equal to the observed one.
This gives a right-tailed p-value


Code
# One-tailed test: is Silver more volatile than Gold?
p_val <- mean(sim_diffs >= obs_diff)

cat("Observed difference in SD (Silver − Gold):", round(obs_diff, 5), "\n")


Observed difference in SD (Silver − Gold): 0.01051 


Code
cat("Approximate one-tailed permutation p-value:",
    format(p_val, scientific = TRUE, digits = 10), "\n")


Approximate one-tailed permutation p-value: 0e+00 


Interpretation:
A very small p-value implies that the observed volatility difference is unlikely under the null —
we have statistical evidence to reject the null hypothesis and conclude that there is in fact a difference in factors driving silver’s volatility and price, that can’t be explained by market randommness or chance.



We now simulate a world where gold and silver have the same true volatility, and check how often our permutation test falsely rejects the null.
This helps us assess how well the test controls the Type I error rate.


Code
simulate_type1_error <- function(n = 30, reps = 20, perms = 100, seed = 47) {
  set.seed(seed)  # ensures reproducibility

  p_vals <- numeric(reps)

  for (i in 1:reps) {
    group1 <- rnorm(n)
    group2 <- rnorm(n)

    df <- tibble(
      Return = c(group1, group2),
      Metal = rep(c("Gold", "Silver"), each = n)
    )

    obs_diff <- df |>
      group_by(Metal) |>
      summarise(sd = sd(Return), .groups = "drop") |>
      summarise(diff_sd = diff(sd)) |>
      pull(diff_sd)

    sim_diffs <- replicate(perms, {
      df |>
        mutate(Metal = sample(Metal)) |>
        group_by(Metal) |>
        summarise(sd = sd(Return), .groups = "drop") |>
        summarise(diff_sd = diff(sd)) |>
        pull(diff_sd)
    })

    p_vals[i] <- mean(sim_diffs >= obs_diff)
  }

  mean(p_vals < 0.05)
}

simulate_type1_error(n = 30, reps = 20, perms = 100)


[1] 0.05


Interpretation:
This number tells us how often the permutation test incorrectly rejects H₀ when there is no true difference.
While it differs depending on the seed, 47 gives us .05. The test is behaving well, giving us a Type I Error only .05 of the time If much higher or lower, it may be too liberal or conservative under certain conditions.



We now simulate a world where gold and silver have different true volatilities, and check how often our permutation test fails to reject the null.
This helps us assess the Type II error rate — the probability of failing to detect a true difference.


Code
simulate_type2_error <- function(n = 30, reps = 100, perms = 1000, true_diff = 0.2, seed = 47) {
  set.seed(seed) 

  p_vals <- numeric(reps)

  for (i in 1:reps) {
    group1 <- rnorm(n, sd = 1)
    group2 <- rnorm(n, sd = 1 + true_diff)  # Introduce a true difference in volatility

    df <- tibble(
      Return = c(group1, group2),
      Metal = rep(c("Gold", "Silver"), each = n)
    )

    obs_diff <- df |>
      group_by(Metal) |>
      summarise(sd = sd(Return), .groups = "drop") |>
      summarise(diff_sd = diff(sd)) |>
      pull(diff_sd)

    sim_diffs <- replicate(perms, {
      df |>
        mutate(Metal = sample(Metal)) |>
        group_by(Metal) |>
        summarise(sd = sd(Return), .groups = "drop") |>
        summarise(diff_sd = diff(sd)) |>
        pull(diff_sd)
    })

    p_vals[i] <- mean(sim_diffs >= obs_diff)
  }

  mean(p_vals >= 0.05)  # Proportion of times the null is not rejected
}

simulate_type2_error(n = 30, reps = 100, perms = 1000, true_diff = 0.2)


[1] 0.69


Interpretation:
This number tells us that the permutation test fails to reject H₀ 69% of the time when there is in fact a true difference in volatility (with a true difference of 0.2 in standard deviations).

An 69% Type II error rate indicates that the test has low power under these conditions. This could be due to factors such as:

Small sample size (n = 30): With limited data, it becomes harder to detect subtle differences.
Subtle true difference (true_diff = 0.2): The actual difference in volatility may not be large enough to be consistently detected.
Number of repetitions (reps = 20): Increasing the number of repetitions could provide a more reliable estimate of the error rate.

To reduce the Type II error rate and increase the power of the test, one could:

Increase the sample size (n).
Increase the number of permutations (perms).
Use a larger true difference (true_diff) to simulate more pronounced effects.




This simulation-based test supports the claim that silver exhibits greater daily price variability than gold from August to October 2025. This difference in volatility, measured by standard deviation, provides evidence of distinct underlying factors driving the prices of these metals.
Given the industrial use of silver and its smaller market size, this result aligns with economic intuition: silver’s price tends to react more sharply to both macroeconomic shocks and commodity supply disruptions.
The one-tailed permutation test not only confirms this difference but does so without relying on normality assumptions, making it a robust method for comparing volatility in real-world financial data.

title: Permutation Testing: Are Silver’s Factors the same as Gold (August–October)?
text:
We compare the standard deviations of daily log returns for gold and silver.

Null Hypothesis (H₀): Gold and Silver are driven by the same market factors and their difference in volatility can be explained by chance and randomness of markets.
Alternative Hypothesis (H₁): The underlying market dynamics for Silver lead to greater daily price variability than Gold that can’t be explained by randomness or chance.

Instead of relying on a theoretical distribution (like a t-test or F-test), we use a permutation test, which simulates what differences we’d expect purely due to chance under the null hypothesis.
We look at market volatility and price to conclude wether or not these two metals are influenced by the same factors.

title: Permutation Testing: Are Silver’s Factors the same as Gold (August–October)?
text:
We use quantmod to pull daily futures data from Yahoo Finance and tidyverse for data wrangling. Log returns are computed using dailyReturn(..., type = "log") for stability and symmetry.


Code
library(quantmod)
library(tidyverse)
library(infer)
library(purrr)

# Download daily futures prices (August 2025 – Present)
GC_F <- getSymbols("GC=F", src = "yahoo", from = "2025-08-01", to = Sys.Date(), auto.assign = FALSE)
SI_F <- getSymbols("SI=F", src = "yahoo", from = "2025-08-01", to = Sys.Date(), auto.assign = FALSE)

# Compute log returns
gold_ret <- dailyReturn(Cl(GC_F), type = "log")
silver_ret <- dailyReturn(Cl(SI_F), type = "log")

# Combine and reshape to long format
returns_long <- tibble(
  date = index(gold_ret),
  Gold = as.numeric(gold_ret),
  Silver = as.numeric(silver_ret)
) |>
  drop_na() |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(Gold, Silver), names_to = "Metal", values_to = "Return")

title: Permutation Testing: Are Silver’s Factors the same as Gold (August–October)?
text:
This plot shows the raw time series of daily log returns for gold and silver.
It helps the reader visually compare volatility over time — wider fluctuations suggest higher variance.


Code
ggplot(returns_long, aes(x = date, y = Return, color = Metal)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(
    title = "Daily Log Returns for Gold and Silver (August–Present)",
    x = "Date", y = "Daily Log Return",
    color = "Metal"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









Analysis:
We observe that silver (in blue) exhibits more frequent and larger daily swings compared to gold.
This visual pattern suggests that silver may indeed have higher volatility — which we now test more formally.

title: Permutation Testing: Are Silver’s Factors the same as Gold (August–October)?
Permutation Testing: Are Silver’s Factors the same as Gold (August–October)?
Permutation Testing: Are Silver’s Factors the same as Gold (August–October)?
text:
We repeat the permutation process 1000 times using map_dbl() to create a distribution of SD differences expected under the null hypothesis (i.e., if metals were equally volatile). The map_() function allows us to repeat this process smoothly and efficiently.


Code
set.seed(47)

# Perform 1000 permutations under the null hypothesis
sim_diffs <- map_dbl(1:1000, ~simulate_diff_sd(returns_long))

# Store in tibble for plotting
sim_df <- tibble(diff_sd = sim_diffs)

title: Permutation Testing: Are Silver’s Factors the same as Gold (August–October)?
text:
This histogram shows the null distribution of SD differences.
The red dashed line represents the actual observed difference.
If it’s far to the right, that provides evidence that silver is truly more volatile.


Code
ggplot(sim_df, aes(x = diff_sd)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "skyblue", color = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = obs_diff, color = "red", linetype = "dashed", size = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Permutation Distribution of Difference in Volatility (Silver − Gold)",
    subtitle = "Red dashed line shows the observed difference",
    x = "Difference in SD under Null (permuted)",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









Interpretation:
The observed value lies far in the upper tail of the distribution — suggesting that such a large difference would be very unlikely by chance if the metals were equally volatile.

title: Permutation Testing: Are Silver’s Factors the same as Gold (August–October)?
text:
We calculate the proportion of permuted SD differences that are greater than or equal to the observed one.
This gives a right-tailed p-value


Code
# One-tailed test: is Silver more volatile than Gold?
p_val <- mean(sim_diffs >= obs_diff)

cat("Observed difference in SD (Silver − Gold):", round(obs_diff, 5), "\n")


Observed difference in SD (Silver − Gold): 0.01051 


Code
cat("Approximate one-tailed permutation p-value:",
    format(p_val, scientific = TRUE, digits = 10), "\n")


Approximate one-tailed permutation p-value: 0e+00 


Interpretation:
A very small p-value implies that the observed volatility difference is unlikely under the null —
we have statistical evidence to reject the null hypothesis and conclude that there is in fact a difference in factors driving silver’s volatility and price, that can’t be explained by market randommness or chance.

title: ECON 091 Final Project: Cook County Sentencing
text:
1(b) Describing the data
https://datacatalog.cookcountyil.gov/Legal-Judicial/Sentencing/tg8v-tm6u/about_data
The data used in this project comes from the Cook County Sentencing dataset, published on the Cook County Open Data Portal and maintained by the Cook County State’s Attorney’s Office (CCSAO), in fact, the office in which my Mother, Catherine Goldhaber used to work in. Cook is a large county in Illinois that covers the Chicagoland area and all of its surrounding suburbs. The dataset consists of administrative court records documenting sentences imposed following convictions in Cook County, Illinois. Each observation corresponds to a single charged offense that received a sentence, rather than a unique individual, meaning defendants with multiple sentenced charges may appear multiple times in the data.
The data are collected as part of the county’s routine judicial and prosecutorial record-keeping process, and republished quarterly. Information is recorded during the normal processing of criminal cases and then released publicly to promote transparency in the criminal justice system.
The dataset is not randomly sampled. Instead, it represents a population dataset of sentencing events recorded by Cook County courts over the covered time period. As a result, inference from this dataset should be interpreted as descriptive of Cook County sentencing practices rather than as estimates from a random sample.
There are several important potential sources of bias. First, the dataset includes only cases that resulted in a sentence, excluding arrests that did not lead to conviction, cases dismissed, or cases resolved through diversion programs. This creates selection bias, as the data reflect later stages of the criminal justice process rather than initial charging or policing decisions. Second, key determinants of sentencing—such as prior criminal history, plea bargaining details, sentencing guideline ranges, quality of legal representation, and evidentiary strength—are not fully observed, raising concerns about omitted variable bias in regression analysis. Third, demographic variables such as race and gender may be subject to misclassification or missingness, as they are administratively.
In terms of representativeness, the dataset is representative of sentenced cases in Cook County, but it is not representative of all criminal defendants, all arrests, or sentencing practices outside Cook County. Because Cook County is a large and diverse urban jurisdiction, patterns observed here may differ substantially from those in suburban, rural, or out-of-state courts. Consequently, conclusions from this analysis should be interpreted as applying specifically to Cook County sentencing outcomes, rather than to the U.S. criminal justice system as a whole.

text:
1(c) Descriptive stats table & what stands out


Show code
# Q1(c): Clean data and create variables (including outcome)
# Outcome = years_sentenced
 

sent_clean <- sent |>
  # your intended analytic sample
  filter(current_sentence_flag == TRUE, primary_charge_flag == TRUE) |>
  mutate(
    # numeric coercions
    charge_count = suppressWarnings(as.numeric(charge_count)),
    length_of_case_in_days = suppressWarnings(as.numeric(length_of_case_in_days)),
    age_at_incident = suppressWarnings(as.numeric(age_at_incident)),

    # parse commitment term into a numeric when possible
    commitment_term_num = readr::parse_number(as.character(commitment_term)),

    # Convert commitment to DAYS (keep only true time units)
    commitment_days = case_when(
      commitment_unit == "Hours"   ~ commitment_term_num / 24,
      commitment_unit == "Days"    ~ commitment_term_num,
      commitment_unit == "Weeks"   ~ commitment_term_num * 7,
      commitment_unit == "Months"  ~ commitment_term_num * 30.44,
      commitment_unit == "Year(s)" ~ commitment_term_num * 365,
      TRUE                         ~ NA_real_
    ),

    # outcome in years
    years_sentenced = commitment_days / 365,

    # Clean race (regex approach; keep Missing/Other buckets)
    race_clean = case_when(
      is.na(race) ~ "Missing",
      str_detect(race, regex("^black$", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Black",
      str_detect(race, regex("^white\\b", ignore_case = TRUE)) &
        str_detect(race, regex("hispanic|latino", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "White (Hispanic/Latino)",
      str_detect(race, regex("^white$", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "White",
      str_detect(race, regex("hispanic|latino", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Hispanic/Latino",
      str_detect(race, regex("asian", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Asian",
      str_detect(race, regex("american\\s*indian", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "American Indian",
      str_detect(race, regex("unknown", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Missing",
      TRUE ~ "Other/Unknown"
    ),

    # Clean gender
    gender_clean = case_when(
      is.na(gender) ~ "Missing",
      str_detect(gender, regex("^male$", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Male",
      str_detect(gender, regex("^female$", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Female",
      str_detect(gender, regex("unknown", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Other/Unknown",
      TRUE ~ "Other/Unknown"
    ),

    # Judge: many categories -> lump to avoid an enormous model
    judge_clean = fct_explicit_na(factor(sentence_judge), na_level = "Missing"),
    judge_group = fct_lump_n(judge_clean, n = 25, other_level = "Other judges"),

    # Controls you already started using (lump levels to keep model stable)
    offense_group = fct_lump_n(factor(updated_offense_category), n = 15, other_level = "Other offenses"),
    disp_class_group = fct_lump_n(factor(disposition_charged_class), n = 10, other_level = "Other/Unknown"),
    sentence_type = fct_explicit_na(factor(sentence_type), na_level = "Missing"),

    # Time: sentencing year can matter (policy/guideline changes over time)
    sentence_date_parsed = parse_date_time(as.character(sentence_date), orders = "Y b d I:M:S p", tz = "UTC"),
    sentencing_year = factor(year(sentence_date_parsed)),
    sentencing_year_num = year(sentence_date_parsed),

    # Modeling convenience: log transform for skew
    log_years = log1p(years_sentenced)
  ) |>
  # keep rows where key analysis variables exist
  filter(
    !is.na(years_sentenced),
    years_sentenced >= 0,
    years_sentenced <= 200,              # trims extreme likely entry issues; still allows very long sentences
    !is.na(age_at_incident),
    !is.na(charge_count),
    !is.na(length_of_case_in_days),
    !is.na(offense_group),
    !is.na(disp_class_group),
    !is.na(sentence_type)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    # reference categories for interpretability
    race_clean = fct_relevel(factor(race_clean), "White"),
    gender_clean = fct_relevel(factor(gender_clean), "Male"),
    sentence_type = fct_relevel(sentence_type, "Probation")
  )

Of the continuious variables, the average sentence length is about 2.89 years, with a wide range from 0 to 189 years (after cleaning). The average age at incident is around 33 years old. The average charge count is about 1 charges per case, and the average length of case in days is 291 days.

section: 1(d) Is there reason to believe you have a good research question? (t-test)
text:
1(d) Is there reason to believe you have a good research question? (t-test)


Show code
# Q1(d): t-test (simple evidence of group differences)
 

bw <- sent_clean |>
  filter(race_clean %in% c("Black","White"))

t.test(log_years ~ race_clean, data = bw)



    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  log_years by race_clean
t = -18.18, df = 42716, p-value < 2.2e-16
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group White and group Black is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -0.06512863 -0.05245231
sample estimates:
mean in group White mean in group Black 
           1.137761            1.196552 


This T-test is solely motivational, as the research question focuses on the overall modeling of sentence length, but this t test provides preliminary evidence that there are indeed differences in sentencing outcomes by race, one of the key demographic variables of interest in the research question.
Q1(d) Interpretation:
The two-sample t-test comparing mean log sentence length between Black and White defendants shows a highly statistically significant difference (t = −18.18, p < 2.2e−16). The null hypothesis of equal means is therefore decisively rejected. The estimated 95% confidence interval for the difference in mean log sentence length (White − Black) is [−0.065, −0.052], which does not include zero.
The sample means indicate that Black defendants have a higher average log sentence length (mean = 1.196) than White defendants (mean = 1.138). This unadjusted difference suggests a meaningful disparity in sentencing outcomes by race in the raw data.
While this result is purely descriptive and not prepared to extraploate, it provides strong motivation for a more careful modeling approach that controls for legally relevant factors such as offense type, charge class, sentence type, and judge. The presence of a large and statistically precise difference at this preliminary stage supports the validity of the research question and suggests that race, alongside judicial assignment and case characteristics, may play an important role in predicting sentence length.

title: ECON 091 Final Project: Cook County Sentencing
text:
2(a) Hypothesis and expected trend
Null (H0):
After accounting for legally relevant case characteristics and the inclusion of judge identity as a predictor, sentence length is fully explained by observable case factors, and defendant race and gender have no additional association with years sentenced.
Hypothesis (H1):
Even after accounting for legally relevant case characteristics and including judge identity as a predictor, sentence length is not fully explained by case factors alone, and defendant race and gender remain systematically associated with years sentenced.
Expected trends (why):
- Judge identity is incorporated by including judge-group indicator variables, where the most frequent judges are kept as their own categories and low-volume judges are pooled into an “Other judges” group to keep the model stable and interpretable.

Race/gender differences could appear due to a mix of case selection into categories not fully observed (priors, plea decisions, guideline departures) and/or differential treatment—this project is descriptive/predictive, not causal.

title: ECON 091 Final Project: Cook County Sentencing
text:
2(b) Model Specification, why this trend?
I model sentence length in years on a log scale:
\[
\log(1+\text{years}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1(\text{race}) + \beta_2(\text{gender}) + \beta_3(\text{judge}) + \beta X + \varepsilon
\] – This formula visualization is generated by AI.
The primary explanatory variables of interest are judge identity, defendant race, and defendant gender, as these factors capture both institutional discretion and demographic characteristics that prior research and public discussion suggest may be associated with sentencing outcomes. The model also includes legally relevant control variables—offense category, charged offense class, sentence type, number of charges, defendant age at the incident, and length of the case—which are expected to systematically influence sentence severity based on statutory guidelines and case complexity. In some specifications, year fixed effects are included to account for changes in sentencing practices over time due to policy shifts, judicial turnover, or evolving prosecutorial norms.
This functional form is chosen for two main reasons. First, sentence length is highly right-skewed, with a small number of extremely long sentences. Taking the logarithm reduces the influence of these extreme values and helps stabilize the variance of the error term, improving model fit and interpretability. Second, the log specification allows coefficients to be interpreted as approximate percentage differences in sentence length, which is intuitive when comparing outcomes across demographic groups or judges, particularly when baseline sentence lengths vary substantially across cases.
The inclusion of judge fixed effects is motivated by descriptive evidence of substantial variation in average sentence length across judges, suggesting that judicial assignment may meaningfully shape sentencing outcomes beyond case characteristics alone. This variation reflects differences in judicial philosophy, experience, and sentencing discretion, making judge identity a central predictor in a model of sentence length. The inclusion of demographic variables such as race and gender allows the model to test whether observable differences in sentencing persist after conditioning on legally relevant case factors. Overall, the model is designed to capture systematic trends in sentencing outcomes while separating the roles of institutional discretion, case characteristics, and defendant demographics.

title: ECON 091 Final Project: Cook County Sentencing
text:
2(c) What would make you reject/respecify?
I would respecify if: - diagnostics show extreme heteroskedasticity or nonlinearity even on the log scale, - judge categories are too sparse (overfitting); I address this by lumping judges, - out-of-sample error is poor, suggesting missing key predictors or that a different functional form (e.g., Gamma regression) is needed.

# function to compute performance on original YEARS scale
eval_on_years <- function(model, data_df) {
  pred_log <- predict(model, newdata = data_df)
  pred_years <- pmax(0, expm1(pred_log))  # back-transform; enforce nonnegative
  tibble(
    rmse_years = rmse(data_df$years_sentenced, pred_years),
    mae_years  = mae(data_df$years_sentenced, pred_years)
  )
}

In the pred vs. actual plot for model 4, there is an obvious distinction between two groups. I beleive it to be because of mandiatory minimums and maximums for certain offenses that cause clustering of sentences at specific lengths. For example, drug offenses may have mandatory minimum sentences that lead to a concentration of sentences at those lengths, while other offenses may have statutory maximums that cap the length of sentences. This results in a non-linear relationship between predicted and actual sentence lengths, as the model may not fully capture these legal constraints.
Nonetheless, I thought it would be a great opportunity to run k-means clustering, to test its effectiveness in identifying these two regimes in the data, as a way to further explore the model’s performance across different sentence length categories.

title: ECON 091 Final Project: Cook County Sentencing
text:
3(b) Evaluate

3(b)(i) Which model performs best? Why?
Model 4, which includes legally relevant case characteristics, judge identity, and a time trend, performs best according to all evaluation metrics. It achieves the lowest RMSE (2.52 years) and lowest MAE (1.05 years) among the candidate models, indicating the most accurate predictions of sentence length on average. Model 4 also has the highest adjusted R-square (0.631), explaining slightly more variation in sentence length than models that exclude judge or time controls. Although the performance gains over Model 3 are modest, they suggest that accounting for both judicial heterogeneity and temporal variation provides incremental improvements in predictive performance beyond case characteristics alone.
Model 4 performs best primarily because it includes the most information, incorporating legally relevant case characteristics, judge identity, and a time trend. By construction, adding additional predictors to a linear regression model cannot decrease R-sqare and will typically improve in-sample fit, even if the added variables have limited substantive importance. For this reason, the improvement in R-square alone should not be interpreted as strong evidence that the added variables are crucial drivers of sentencing outcomes.


3(b)(ii) Do you trust your model? Why?
I trust the model more as a predictive and descriptive tool than as a causal explanation of sentencing outcomes. It performs well in terms of fit and prediction accuracy, explaining about 63 percent of the variation in log sentence length, and the stability of coefficients across specifications suggests that the results are not driven by simple omitted variable bias from offense type alone. However, several important determinants of sentencing, such as prior criminal history, plea bargaining, sentencing guideline ranges, and quality of legal representation, are not observed in the data and cannot be fully captured by categorical controls. As a result, the estimated race and gender coefficients should be interpreted as conditional associations rather than causal effects.


3(b)(iii) Publication-ready? What would you need?
Not yet. To get closer to publication quality, the analysis would need to incorporate additional key controls such as criminal history, plea versus trial status, and sentencing guideline ranges. It would also benefit from robustness checks using alternative samples, such as restricting to felony cases or specific offense categories. In addition, stronger identification strategies, including approaches that rely on plausibly random judge assignment, would be necessary to support causal interpretation. Finally, fairness focused evaluations, such as comparing prediction errors and calibration across demographic groups, would help assess whether the model performs equitably across populations.

title: ECON 091 Final Project: Cook County Sentencing
text:
4(a) What tests do we need?
For linear regression, common checks include: - ANOVA / nested F-test (e.g., does adding judge improve fit?),

Residuals vs fitted (nonlinearity / heteroskedasticity),
QQ plot (normality of residuals),
Corrplot of numeric predictors (multicollinearity concerns),
Breusch–Pagan test (heteroskedasticity).

4(b) Run them + does the model pass?

The correlation matrix shows no evidence of problematic multicollinearity among the predictors. Aside from the expected high correlation between years_sentenced and log_years, all other correlations are close to zero. This indicates that the model passes the multicollinearity check.


QQ plot


Show code
qqnorm(resid(m4), main = "QQ Plot of Residuals (m4)")
qqline(resid(m4), lty = 2)









The QQ plot shows that the residuals deviate from the reference line in the tails, particularly for large positive residuals, indicating heavier tails than a normal distribution. This suggests that while the residuals are approximately normal in the center, extreme sentencing outcomes are not well captured by the model. Given the large sample size, this deviation is not unexpected and does not invalidate the model, but it reinforces the use of robust standard errors and cautions against relying on normality assumptions for inference.


Heteroskedasticity test


Show code
# Residuals plot
plot(m4$fitted.values, resid(m4),
     xlab = "Fitted values",
     ylab = "Residuals",
     main = "Residuals vs Fitted (m4)")

Show code
# Additional method: This was covered in Foundations of Data Science (Pomona) 
bptest(m4) 



    studentized Breusch-Pagan test

data:  m4
BP = 10932, df = 77, p-value < 2.2e-16


Residual plot: The residuals-versus-fitted plot shows clear evidence of heteroskedasticity, with the variance of residuals increasing at higher fitted values. This pattern is consistent with formal test results and reflects greater uncertainty in predicting long sentences. However, the absence of strong curvature suggests that the log-linear functional form is appropriate for modeling the conditional mean.
B-P Test (Learned in Foundations of Data Science): - The Breusch–Pagan test is a formal statistical test that examines whether the variance of the regression residuals is constant across observations, with a significant result indicating heteroskedasticity.
The studentized Breusch–Pagan test strongly rejects the null hypothesis of homoskedasticity (BP = 10,823, p < 2.2e−16), indicating that the variance of the regression errors is not constant across observations. This confirms the presence of heteroskedasticity in the model, which is consistent with earlier diagnostics showing larger errors for long sentences. As a result, standard OLS standard errors may be unreliable, and the use of heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors is appropriate for inference.


Show code

The nested F-test compares Model 2, which includes legally relevant case characteristics, to Model 3, which additionally includes judge identity as a set of predictors. The test strongly rejects the null hypothesis that the coefficients on the judge indicators are jointly equal to zero (F = 39.12, p < 2.2e−16). This indicates that, taken together, judge identity explains a statistically significant amount of additional variation in log sentence length beyond offense type, charge class, sentence type, and other case characteristics.
Although the increase in overall model fit is modest in magnitude, the highly significant F-statistic reflects both the large sample size and the systematic differences in average sentencing severity across judges. Substantively, this result suggests that judicial assignment is meaningfully associated with sentencing outcomes, even after accounting for legally relevant case factors. However, this finding should be interpreted as descriptive rather than causal, as judge assignment is not assumed to be random and the test does not identify the sources of judicial variation.


Residual Analysis for Zero Conditional Mean Assumption
To test the Zero Conditional Mean (Exogeneity) assumption, we perform a residual analysis. This involves regressing the residuals from the main regression model on the independent variables. If the coefficients in this regression are statistically significant, it suggests that the residuals are correlated with the predictors, indicating a potential violation of the assumption.


Show code
# Fit the main regression model

                   charge_count + length_of_case_in_days +
                   offense_group + disp_class_group + sentence_type +
                   judge_group + sentencing_year_num, data = sent_clean)

# Extract residuals
residuals <- resid(main_model)

# Regress residuals on independent variables
residual_test <- lm(residuals ~ race_clean + gender_clean + age_at_incident +
                      charge_count + length_of_case_in_days +
                      offense_group + disp_class_group + sentence_type +
                      judge_group + sentencing_year_num, data = sent_clean)

# Summary of the residual regression
summary(residual_test)
